page: Microsoft Azure D48ls_v5 is a compute-focused virtual machine instance belonging to the DLSv5 server family. Powered by the Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor, this instance features 48 dedicated vCPUs mapped to 24 physical cores of an Intel Xeon 8370C processor operating at 2.8 GHz. The x86_64 architecture incorporates a cache hierarchy consisting of 48 KB L1d, 1 MB L2, and 48 MB L3 cache. It is configured with 96.0 GB of system memory, yielding a resource density of 2.0 GB of RAM per vCPU. This low-memory configuration is designed for compute-heavy workloads that do not require massive memory footprints.

This instance does not include any local storage, meaning all storage requirements must be met via external network-attached storage, though the instance is premium storage capable. There are no integrated GPUs, making it unsuitable for hardware-accelerated graphics or GPU-bound machine learning training. Additionally, the instance does not include complimentary public IPv4 addresses. These hardware trade-offs position the D48ls_v5 as a highly specialized node where cost efficiency is maximized by stripping out local storage and excess memory in favor of raw CPU allocation.

Benchmark data indicates strong multi-threaded performance, particularly in decompression and cryptographic operations. The instance achieved a strong rating in multi-threaded bzip3 decompression at 221.23 GB/sec and in Passmark CPU encryption at 43318.73. Memory subsystem performance is also notable, with a strong Passmark memory score of 2837.7 and uncached large-block read bandwidth reaching 164.15 GB/sec. For artificial intelligence workloads, the CPU demonstrates strong performance in LLM inference, processing 2539.2 tokens per second for a small 135M model prompt and 199.49 tokens per second for a medium 7B model prompt. Other standard operations, such as Redis SET operations at 44.81 million operations per second and static web serving at 40.11 GB/sec, fall within the average performance tier.

Given its high vCPU count and constrained memory-to-core ratio, the D48ls_v5 is best suited for compute-intensive tasks that fit within a 96 GB memory boundary. Appropriate workloads include multi-threaded compilation, batch processing, CPU-based machine learning inference, web serving, and cryptographic processing. It is also well-suited for distributed compute nodes where state is managed externally.
description: Microsoft Azure D48ls_v5 is a compute-optimized virtual machine instance from the DLSv5 family, featuring 48 dedicated vCPUs and 96.0 GB of RAM. Powered by an Intel Xeon 8370C processor running at 2.8 GHz on the Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor, this x86_64 instance provides a 1:2 vCPU-to-RAM ratio tailored for compute-heavy tasks. The instance lacks local storage and integrated GPUs, relying entirely on external storage options. Performance benchmarks show strong capabilities in multi-threaded bzip3 decompression, CPU encryption, and small-to-medium LLM inference prompt processing. Conversely, database operations and web serving throughput perform at average levels. This server is highly cost-efficient for workloads requiring substantial processing power without the overhead of large memory allocations, making it ideal for software compilation, batch processing, and CPU-based model inference.
og_description: Microsoft Azure D48ls_v5 is a compute-optimized instance with 48 Intel Xeon vCPUs, 96 GB RAM, and no local storage, delivering strong performance in multi-threaded decompression and CPU cryptography.
meta_description: Microsoft Azure D48ls_v5 is a compute-optimized instance featuring 48 Intel Xeon vCPUs and 96 GB RAM, designed for CPU-intensive workloads.
tagline: A compute-optimized, low-memory virtual machine featuring forty-eight dedicated Intel Xeon vCPUs for high-throughput parallel processing and cryptographic workloads.
bullet_points: 48 dedicated Intel Xeon 8370C vCPUs running at 2.8 GHz
96.0 GB of system memory providing a 1:2 vCPU-to-RAM ratio
No bundled local storage or integrated GPU resources
Strong multi-threaded decompression and cryptographic performance
Well-suited for CPU-based LLM inference and software compilation
